name: Wele
description: Wele is the supreme being in Luhya mythology, revered as the creator and sustainer of all things. Wele is often described as a distant and benevolent force, responsible for the creation of the world, humanity, and all living creatures. While not directly involved in the daily affairs of humans, Wele is believed to possess immense power and wisdom, capable of influencing events and providing guidance through intermediaries and natural signs. Some accounts portray Wele as having both male and female aspects, embodying the duality of creation and existence. Wele is not typically represented in physical form, emphasizing the deity's transcendent and intangible nature. Prayers and offerings are sometimes directed toward Wele, particularly during times of crisis or thanksgiving, seeking blessings and protection. The reverence for Wele underscores the Luhya belief in a higher power responsible for the order and harmony of the universe. Wele's influence permeates Luhya culture, shaping moral values and providing a framework for understanding the world and humanity's place within it. Stories and legends about the origins of the world and the first people often invoke Wele as the ultimate source of creation. The concept of Wele represents the Luhya people's understanding of the divine and their connection to the cosmos.
subtitle: The Supreme Creator and Sustainer

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the World
keyMyths.description: Wele created the world from nothing, shaping the land, the sky, and the waters. Then Wele populated it with plants, animals and finally humans.

keyMyths.title: The First People
keyMyths.description: Wele created the first man and woman, gifting them with the ability to cultivate the land and multiply. They were tasked with caring for Wele's creation.

keyMyths.title: Wele's Distance
keyMyths.description: After creation, Wele withdrew from direct involvement, allowing the world to follow its course. This distance emphasizes human responsibility and free will.

sources.author: Osogo, John
sources.description: A comprehensive account of Luhya history and culture, including religious beliefs.
sources.text: A comprehensive account of Luhya history and culture, including religious beliefs.

sources.author: Wandibba, Simiyu
sources.work: Indigenous Knowledge and Beliefs of the Bukusu People
sources.description: Exploration of Bukusu cultural practices and spiritual understanding.
sources.text: Exploration of Bukusu cultural practices and spiritual understanding.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: While direct worship of Wele is rare, offerings of food and drink are sometimes made during important ceremonies and life events as a sign of respect and gratitude for Wele's blessings.

worshipPractices.name: Prayers
worshipPractices.description: Prayers are often directed to Wele during times of crisis or need, seeking guidance, protection, and blessings. These prayers acknowledge Wele's supreme power and benevolent nature.

festivals.name: Harvest Celebrations
festivals.description: Harvest celebrations often include expressions of gratitude to Wele for providing sustenance and ensuring a bountiful harvest. These celebrations reinforce the connection between Wele and the well-being of the community.

festivals.name: Initiation Rites
festivals.description: During initiation rites, elders invoke Wele's name, seeking blessings and guidance for the initiates as they transition into adulthood. This signifies Wele's role in shaping the destiny of individuals and the community.

cultural.modernLegacy: In modern Luhya society, Wele remains a symbol of the traditional belief system and cultural identity. While adherence to traditional practices may vary, the concept of Wele as the supreme creator continues to influence ethical values and spiritual beliefs.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In Luhya mythology, Wele's origins are shrouded in mystery and antiquity. Wele is often depicted as self-existent, without beginning or end. Wele existed before the cosmos, a solitary entity possessing infinite potential. The act of creation itself is seen as an emanation of Wele's divine will, a manifestation of the inherent power contained within this supreme being. There are no specific stories detailing Wele's birth or parentage, as the deity is considered to be the ultimate source of all existence. Wele's existence is accepted as a fundamental truth, a cornerstone of the Luhya worldview. The name 'Wele' itself carries connotations of greatness, power, and the ultimate source of life. Thus, Wele's origin is not a narrative event but rather a statement of being, the eternal foundation upon which all else is built.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: While Wele is not the central character in many specific myths or stories, Wele's influence permeates Luhya narratives. Wele is often invoked as the ultimate authority, the source of justice and cosmic order. Stories might describe Wele intervening indirectly, sending signs or omens to guide humanity. Some narratives recount how Wele created the first humans from clay or other natural materials, bestowing upon them the gift of life and reason. These creation accounts emphasize Wele's role as the benevolent provider and sustainer of humankind. Wele is also seen as the guarantor of moral order, punishing those who violate sacred laws or disrupt the harmony of the community. However, Wele's judgment is often tempered with mercy, offering opportunities for repentance and reconciliation. These stories serve to reinforce the importance of ethical conduct and respect for the divine will.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Direct worship of Wele, in the form of temples or organized priesthoods, is not a prominent feature of Luhya religious practice. Instead, reverence for Wele is expressed through more subtle and integrated ways. Prayers are often directed to Wele during important ceremonies, such as births, marriages, and funerals. These prayers acknowledge Wele's supreme power and seek blessings for the individuals and the community. Libations of beer or offerings of food may be made to honor Wele, particularly during harvest festivals or times of communal thanksgiving. The concept of Wele is also embedded in the moral code and ethical values of the Luhya people. Upholding principles of honesty, justice, and compassion is seen as a way of honoring Wele's divine will. Elders play a crucial role in transmitting these values and ensuring that Wele's presence is acknowledged in daily life.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The concept of Wele continues to exert a significant influence on contemporary Luhya society. While many Luhya people have embraced Christianity or other religions, the traditional belief in Wele persists as a cultural foundation. Wele represents the enduring values of creation, sustenance, and moral order. The idea of a supreme being who is both powerful and benevolent shapes the ethical framework of the community. Even those who identify with other faiths may still invoke Wele's name in times of crisis or need, drawing upon the deep-seated belief in Wele's power to provide guidance and protection. The legacy of Wele is also reflected in Luhya art, music, and oral traditions. Stories and songs that celebrate the creation of the world and the origins of humanity often reference Wele, ensuring that the deity's presence remains a vital part of the cultural landscape.

shortDescription: Wele is the supreme creator deity in Luhya mythology, responsible for the creation of the world and all living things, revered for immense power and wisdom.
